speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Andrea Brasg. I'm conducting an interview with Henia Bryer. The date is the 15th of November, 1995. The interview is being conducted in Bloemfontein, South Africa, in English; tape four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one quick thing. When you-- you tend to go quiet when you asking a question in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just be aware of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I actually meant to ask you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah. OK. OK. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you were on the way to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were walking through the forest, and it was night. And we were walking what seemed to be a long time. And we arrived at the gates of the camp early in the morning. And what greeted us there was a-- a-- a scene of total desolation. It was like one of those futuristic horror movies where everything is burnt-out and burnt. There is nothing to see, really-- only heaps of corpses of dead people. And this was the picture that greeted us when we arrived in Bergen-Belsen. We were taken straight to barracks. There was no-- no bath there, no-- no-- no selection-- nothing. We were taken to the barracks, and packed in the barracks. In the barracks there was no bunk. There was nothing. There were no blankets, there were-- it was just the floor. And they-- and we somehow landed with-- with-- with people we didn't know. That's my friend and I. There were a few people from my town. But, I don't know how we got lost. Perhaps, during the journey with the-- and there were a lot of Hungarian women that came from Hungary. There were a lot of other people whom we didn't know. But, anyway, they packed us there into these blocks. They were big, enormous blocks. And I remember that in the block that I was in, there were 700 women. To start with, there was only standing place. There was no place to lie down, or to rest, or to do anything at all. And conditions-- they were just appalling. There was no food-- very little of it. There was nothing to do, either. We just stood in the morning for hours and hours to be counted on this appellplatz. And it was still very cold. This is a-- a northern part of Europe. It-- it was-- it was January. It was very cold-- snow. And-- and conditions were just too terrible. We got very meager rations. There was soup boiled with cabbage that was-- you-- you couldn't even look at it. It was so terrible. But I suppose one had to eat it, because otherwise you had nothing, and you died. And, really, people died. People died there. They were just dropping like flies. And every now and then they used to carry them out and just leave them outside. And that-- that was it. And one night my-- my boots were stolen. I don't know who took them. Somebody took them. I probably took them off and put them under my-- my head. Because as the people were dying, so there was more place in the-- in the barrack. You could actually, eventually, lie down on the floor-- bare floor. And so I had to stand in the snow barefoot, for hours, and hours, and hours. And the whole time that I was there, it's just like-- like I was-- and I was also sick with typhus. So that was-- it's like a-- like a dream, like a bad dream. I remember scenes, and then I don't remember. I was desperately ill. And I remember my friend used to take snow from outside and bring it to me to drink, because there was nothing else. She used to melt the snow, and-- and that was-- that was what I was drinking. Because you-- you burnt up with fever. And-- and there was-- it was just-- this was the worst of the lot. And-- and thousands and thousands of people died there. There was just nothing to-- to-- to brighten up your day. 'Cause in the other camps, we still sort of tried to keep-- to keep busy. We sort of-- sometimes we were singing in the evening. We got together with friends. Sometimes we were reciting poetry. And everyone used to add a little bit if somebody forgot. We used to keep yourself sort of occupied or interested in something, so that the gloom would lift a little bit from our lives of everyday. But Bergen-Belsen was just-- it was-- there was no hope there. People really died in the thousands, and they were just left there. And that was-- that was Bergen-Belsen. And, unfortunately, we were-- we were liberated very late-- only in April, 1955. The British Army came in, and the tanks came first. And they had one look at the camp. And they went out again. Because there was disease and all these rotting bodies outside. And then a-- a couple of days later-- we actually stayed on our own for-- for a day or two, maybe three days, where the Germans have left. They-- they sort of mostly ran away. The English have been and have gone. And-- and we were there on our own, sitting outside and just waiting for somebody to come and do something. And eventually, the British sent likes an-- like an auxiliary force. They were probably male nurses and one or two doctors, but not nearly enough for-- for a camp of these dimensions. And-- and they brought food, which was, of course, the wrong food, because people were starving. And-- and they brought this-- this British rations that they gave the soldiers-- a lot of fat food. And people used to get dysentery and-- and died. A lot of people died, actually, after the liberation-- a lot of them. And then they quarantined the whole camp, because there was typhus. And we were very upset about it, and we were very cross. We said that they're afraid that the-- the Germans will get sick from us. That's why they're keeping us here. But they kept us still in the camp. And I couldn't move, in any case. I was very ill, and my feet were in a terrible state. They were swollen and-- a lot of pain. They were-- severe, severe frostbite. And so we stayed there in Bergen-Belsen-- still after the liberation. I stayed for-- for about two or three months, at least-- till the middle of summer. And then people started to-- coming from other camps. They started to come and bring us news from other places. And that's where I heard the news that my mother was alive. Somebody went to Radom and came back. They used to travel and give news of different places. And they told me that the people from Radom, most of them, those who-- who survived, are in Stuttgart. So I went to Stuttgart, there. A friend came to fetch me, and we went by train. And we went to Stuttgart. And there I met a lot of people I knew. They were all from my hometown. There was a street which they requisitioned for us in Stuttgart, on-- on the hill there. And the UNRRA was keeping that DP camp-- the displaced persons camp. And UNRRA is the United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration. And they gave-- they had a kitchen going for us. They supplied us with clothes and with-- with food. And we stayed there-- and-- mostly looking-- looking for relatives, news of relatives-- who remained alive, and who did not. And there were people traveling. Those who were in good condition, physically, used to travel back to Poland and came back to Stuttgart with news. And there, eventually, my mother came to Stuttgart as well. I sent news with somebody who was going back to Poland that I am in Stuttgart, and she and a cousin of mine came and joined me there. And we stayed still for a-- a couple of months. I can't remember how long. But in '40-- beginning of '46, we went to Paris. My uncle live-- I had a uncle who lived in Paris. And he sent a car for us. So we went to-- to Paris, and we stayed with him there till-- well, not all the time, but when-- when we came to Paris, we stayed with-- with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard from your younger brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we didn't hear yet. But eventually, we did hear that he was already in Israel. He was taken with all the youngsters, with the [NON-ENGLISH] Project, that she took all the-- she collected all the youngsters from the camps in Europe and took them to Israel. And he was on a kibbutz in Israel at that stage. But we didn't see him for two years since the war, because we couldn't get to Palestine. My mother decided whether she should go. She had parents there, and brothers, and a sister. And we decided that we should go to Israel. We had family there. And my brother was there, my younger brother was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to wait. So in the meantime, we were in Paris. And then I was sent. But I went to school there. I went everyday, in the evening, for four hours. And then I went for a couple of months, to a-- a special, like a colony for all the-- the children of the French people who were deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow I got in with them. I was the only foreigner. They were all French teenagers, more or less my age, a little younger, a little older. And we went to Nice. And we stayed there for a couple of months, which was very nice to recuperate, because I was with young people. And we did a lot of hiking. And it was-- it was a good thing after these camps to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you integrate with all of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. I mean, there was a-- a language barrier. But I could make myself understood by then. I went to school in Paris, so I could speak, sort of-- more or less. And at-- you-- you know, when you're young, you mix very easily with everybody. And their parents were-- all the-- all the-- the boys and girls that were with me, their parents were all-- also in camps. And most of them didn't come back. So we had a lot in common too. And we stayed there, and then we came back to Paris. And then we eventually made it to Marseilles, to wait for our turn to go to-- to Palestine-- in those days. But it-- it-- we had to wait for a long time. We only got to-- to Israel in '47. That's two years after the war. Excuse me. Because, in those days, as you know, the English wouldn't let Jews in. If you went there, you were sent to Cyprus in another camp, another concentration camp. Well, we called it a concentration camp. Of course, it wasn't, but to us, it was. And so we came to-- to Haifa in 1947. And we joined the family. It was-- my grandparents were there. And I had four uncles and an aunt with-- they all had children. And obviously, they were very happy to see the two of us. And that was when we met my brother for the first time-- two years after the war. And then we got him out of the-- of the kibbutz. He was in a kibbutz in [NON-ENGLISH] in the Hula Valley. And we lived in a very, very modest, simple, little place, and-- all three of us. And my mother had to work. She's never worked in her life before-- I mean, before the war. During the war, she did. She opened a canteen at the police station, and she was actually doing very well. And soon afterwards, the-- the war of liberation started. And I went into the army. And then my brother, afterwards, also went into the army. And I was in the Israeli Army from '48 till '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was wonderful, actually. It was-- this was the way to integrate. I met all the young people. And when I came to Israel, I had a background of Hebrew. But I still went to school, as well, to night school. And I studied. And so you learned to speak Hebrew. I was in a air force camp on Mount Carmel. It-- it's-- this was called Stella Maris. It doesn't exist anymore, but it was there. And we worked. We first-- we were on duty with the radar-- very primitive sort of thing. There were no planes. There was not really an air force, in those days. But we were-- we were at the air force base. And then from there, I was transferred, after about a year, I think. I was there. I was transferred to another unit in Haifa, in another place, with the-- with the Israeli army supplies head office. And I worked in that head office as a soldier. And then when I finished the army, they asked me to stay on as a civilian. It was very interesting, nice work. We had to work out all the rations for all the units, for all the army units, and navy, and air force. We knew exactly where everybody was. So it was quite-- it was responsible work, and it was a state secret. We-- we couldn't tell anything where these units were and what. But we work-- I worked in the office there. And I worked there all the time till I left. We were-- I was in Israel for 5 and 1/2 years, nearly 6 years all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose to join the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, everybody had to. And I would have volunteered in any case. It was the best thing for me. It-- it gave me back my pride in being Jewish. I can't tell you what a feeling it was to wear that uniform after all that we went through, and to be able to walk tall and proud in the streets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we marched in that first-- first anniversary of the Jewish state, we marched in that parade. And we all cried. It was wonderful. It was the best thing that ever happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you would survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were times when there were great doubts. There were really times where-- but I never gave up hope. I used to pray a lot and-- and argue with God. And I used to say to myself, I said, I'm not ready to die yet. I'm too young. I've still got a lot of things to do. And I've still got a lot of things to experience. And I-- I-- I'm not ready yet. And I don't think I ever really was ready and gave up. Because a lot of people did. And-- and-- and that was the worst thing. I remember there was a-- a-- a young Hungarian girl next to me in that barrack in Bergen-Belsen, and she was crying. And she was-- the whole-- she was sitting next to me and crying. I don't want to die, I don't want to die. And I kept on saying to her, but don't talk about it. Just forget about it. Just concentrate. Save your strength. Save your strength. You're using up energy. Don't cry. Stop it. She went on the whole night. And she died that morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when and where did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we married in Israel. I met-- I met Maurice in-- in Israel. He came for a trip. He had-- he had-- funny enough, he had relatives that were connected with my family. A cousin of mine, in Israel, married a Bryer. It was a distant relation of Maurice's father. And that's how we met. I came to visit to her, and he came to visit her. And we met there. And then we started going out. And Maurice actually stayed in Israel. He got a job, because I wasn't ready to leave my family and come to South Africa. And I wasn't sure about it. Eventually, we got married. And then we came to South Africa for one year. That was the condition. Well, it stretched to what seems like forever. And I've been in Bloemfontein every since. 1952 we got married. And a few months later we came to Bloemfontein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your mother and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother stayed on in Israel, and my brother stayed on in Israel. And they-- they lived there. My brother finished the technical on there. He's an architect. And my mother lived there. But we saw each other every year. She either came to visit us here, or I came to her. And we usually spent two or three months together. And then, at the end, when she was too old to live on her own, then I brought her here. And she passed away here eight years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned in the beginning how your grandparents were in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet up with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were still there when we came. They were very, very religious people. My grandfather was a very, very religious man. And he made a special prayer over us in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was-- he couldn't believe when he saw us. He said he wouldn't let us just come near him until he said the special prayer. And it was quite a reunion. You can imagine. I also met relatives that I never knew. All my mother's brothers left Poland, I think, before I was born, and I didn't know them. But they were all very good. And they all, you know, took the place of my father. I had four fathers there. Everybody was giving me advice, and everybody was trying to look after me-- [LAUGHS] all my uncles. And it was great. Israel was wonderful. I would never have left if I didn't get married to a South African.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have two sons. And they're both in Cape Town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allen is the older one, and Dan is the younger one. They're both married. They each have two children-- a boy and a girl each. And as you can guess, my grandchildren are the light of my life. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you relate the Holocaust, or how did you deal with it towards your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know that I was right or wrong, but I didn't talk about it much. If I was asked, I answered. But I-- I didn't. I didn't. Maybe-- I don't know if it was the right thing to do or the wrong thing to do. But I read a lot about children of survivors were affected very badly by their parents' experience. There were second and third-generation Holocaust syndromes they were writing about. And I didn't want this to affect my children in any way. Look, they're well-informed, grown up people today, and obviously, they know what happened. But children tend to be very protective about their parents. And I didn't want them to feel that they should treat me differently because I went through all these things. And I didn't want to treat them differently. I-- I spoke to children of some of my friends, and they were affected by it. They were told every time that if they wouldn't finish a-- a meal that, you know, you-- you have to eat it, because, you know, we were starving-- that sort of thing. And I don't know. You know, everybody try and does their best. But I didn't talk much about it. I must be honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do you stand religiously today?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same as before-- you know, not very observant, I must say, but very traditional. We observe all the festivals. We go to shul whenever we can. And, as-- as you know, I'm a Hebrew teacher. So it's part of my job to hold up the practice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message you'd like to give the world-- to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who am I to give messages to the world? I'm happy that I survived, that I could, in my own way, deal with the problems that arose afterwards. And I only hope that these things never, ever happen again. That's the message. Because it-- it-- besides the people that-- that lost their lives, we-- we are all affected by it. And we're not the same people, we're not-- that we were. Everyone is-- is badly damaged in one way or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to do this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt I owed it to, not so much to my children-- maybe my grandchildren. Because my children are well-informed. They know what happened. But I felt I had to do it for my brother that was killed, for my sister, for my father, and for all the other people who can't talk today and never will be able to tell their story. That is why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents, my mother and my father, just before they got married. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how old your mother was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she was about 30-- something like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were engaged for a long time. He wasn't at home. He was in the army, in Russia. And then he came back. And they got married. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He must have been about 35 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] OK. Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's my mother with her four children. I'm on the top right, there with that navy-blue bow on my head. My younger brother is also on the top. And at the bottom is my little sister on the right side, and my older brother on the left side. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] That was taken before the war. I-- I'm not sure. I must have been 9 or 10 at the time-- maybe less. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was taken before the war, on our last holiday, the whole family there in the forest. My mother is on the top with my older brother on her left side. Next to me is my younger brother. And there's my father with my little sister. [PAUSES FOR 47 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture, and when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was taken after the war-- my mother and I-- in Paris-- when we arrived there. [PAUSES FOR 29 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Henia, tell me about these photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- this was my passport photo when I arrived in Israel-- 1947. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] They were false papers, but we got in anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] And this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That one, that's the army girl-- 1948, Israeli army. It's not in focus. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in the backyard of-- of where we lived there, when I-- I came home for the-- for-- for Saturday, or a day, or something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about this medal?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the badges, my badge from the army, and the ribbon for being in the army during the war of liberation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What does this mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means a lot. That's why I kept it all this time. I'm very proud of it. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Henia what is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of our wedding photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just gonna catch it quickly. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Henia, what is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's one of our wedding pictures. There's Maurice, my mother, myself, and Maurice's father, who came from South Africa for the wedding, in Israel, 1952. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Henia, who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my firstborn son, Allen, and his wife, Denise, at their wedding. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in Cape Town, and their wedding was also in Cape Town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's Richard, their son, our first grandchild-- grandson. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's eight now. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is his little sister, Jenna. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] She's about a year and a 1/2 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Henia, tell me about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a wedding picture of my youngest son, Dan, and his wife, Rebecca. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: They also live in Cape Town. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's Gregory, their very-- their little boy, who is now 4 and 1/2. That was taken a little while ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's Megan, his little sister. And she's nearly two. [PAUSES FOR 58 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is my husband, Maurice. We were married in Israel, in 1952. And we came to South Africa a few months later. And we lived in Bloemfontein every since. We're still here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Up until I met my wife in 1951, I was never emotionally involved in the Holocaust. To me, it was a tragic event which had taken place in a distant country. And only when we met, and my wife confided in me, and told me of her past tragic life, that I became emotionally involved. It is when one hears the personal experience of an individual that one gets involved in the whole tragic circumstances. Since after that, I became more interested in reading about the Holocaust and became involved in it, until we came to South Africa-- which also was a traumatic experience for my wife, not knowing the language, and being in a strange country, not knowing anybody. It was a-- a trying time. She had vivid nightmares of the Holocaust. And it was terrifying to hear her scream at night, waking up, and I-- in which I had to wake her up cautiously from her terrible nightmare. However, over the past 50 years, she can now look dispassionately on it all, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not dispassionately.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and discuss the circumstances with individually anybody, now. And I-- and I think this-- the-- the-- the record, now of-- of what transpired, is a wonderful thing that is being preserved for all time.
